In John 6, Jesus performs a miracle that should have opened His disciples' eyes to who He is—but instead it reveals how easily we settle for lesser things. He feeds five thousand with five loaves and two fish, and the people's response is telling: they want to make Him king by force. They've seen His power to satisfy their hunger, and that's enough for them. But Jesus withdraws from the crowd, sensing something dangerous in their worship. They're drawn to what He can give them, not to who He is. This is the deepest temptation of faith—to want Jesus for His benefits rather than for Himself. The disciples face their own crisis that night, rowing through darkness and wind, terrified when they see Him walking on water. Even after the multiplication of loaves, they don't recognize Him in the storm. Our faith is often like this: we believe when we see the provision, but fear returns the moment circumstances turn uncertain.

What follows is Jesus's most confrontational teaching about what He actually offers. The crowd follows Him to Capernaum, asking when He arrived, and Jesus cuts through their pretense. He knows they're after Him because they ate and were satisfied—not because they understood the sign. He redirects them toward food that endures to eternal life, and they respond by asking what work God requires. It's a reasonable question, but Jesus's answer strips away every human effort: "The work of God is this: to believe in the One He has sent." He's not calling them to accomplish something; He's calling them to receive someone. When they ask for a sign and reference the manna their fathers ate in the wilderness, Jesus reveals something staggering. It wasn't Moses who gave them that bread—it was His Father. And now His Father has sent something far greater: not bread from heaven, but the bread of God Himself, who comes down and gives life to the world.

Then Jesus says it plainly: "I am the bread of life." This isn't metaphor meant to comfort; it's a claim to be the very source of life itself. To believe in Him is to feed on Him, to be sustained by Him in a way that transcends physical hunger and thirst. But the crowd doesn't celebrate this revelation—they grumble. How can Jesus claim to have come down from heaven when they know His parents? The scandal of the Incarnation remains: God in human flesh, offering Himself as sustenance, and it offends human reason. Jesus doesn't soften the claim. Instead He makes it more severe. No one can come to Him unless the Father draws them. Belief isn't a human achievement; it's the Father's gift. And here's the promise that arrests everything: "I will raise him up at the last day." This isn't just about spiritual comfort in the present. Jesus is promising resurrection—bodily, eternal, certain.

When Jesus declares that He will give His flesh for the life of the world, the disciples themselves begin to object. "How can this man give us His flesh to eat?" Yet Jesus goes deeper still, speaking of eating His flesh and drinking His blood. This language, to Jewish ears, was profoundly disturbing—and Jesus doesn't smooth it over. He insists on it: "Unless you eat the flesh and drink the blood of the Son of Man, you have no life in you." This isn't mere symbolism; it's a claim about the absolute necessity of union with Him. To feed on Christ is to abide in Him, and to abide in Him is to live because of Him, just as He lives because of the Father. The promise is staggering in its intimacy and its finality: whoever eats this bread will live forever. Not because they're good enough, not because they've earned it, but because they've received Him. This chapter leaves us with a choice we cannot avoid: Will we come to Jesus for what He gives us, or will we come to Jesus as the gift Himself?
